title: Comprehensions & Lambda
subtitle: Turn long, clunky loops into short, beautiful one-liners — the Pythonic way.
goal: Use list and dict comprehensions, write tiny lambda functions, and replace map/filter with clean comprehensions.

blocks.heading: Welcome to pro-level Python ✨
blocks.html:
You already know how to write loops. Today you learn how to write short, elegant loops that real Python pros use every day. We call this style Pythonic — code that is clean, clear, and just a little bit magic. 🪄

By the end of today you'll turn five-line loops into one beautiful line. Same result, way less typing.

blocks.heading: The long way vs the short way
blocks.html:
Say we have some numbers and we want to double each one. The old way uses a loop and an empty list:

That works! But Python has a shortcut called a list comprehension that does the same thing in one line. Watch. 👇

blocks.caption: Before (long) and after (Pythonic)
blocks.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]

# The long way: loop + empty list + append
doubled_long = []
for x in nums:
    doubled_long.append(x * 2)
print("long way: ", doubled_long)

# The short way: a list comprehension
doubled_short = [x * 2 for x in nums]
print("short way:", doubled_short)
blocks.explain: Both print the same list [2, 4, 6, 8]. The comprehension [x * 2 for x in nums] reads almost like English: 'x times 2, for each x in nums'.

blocks.html: Read it out loud: [x * 2 for x in nums] = "do x times 2 for every x in nums." The recipe comes first, the loop comes after.

blocks.heading: Adding a condition (a filter) 🔎
blocks.html: You can add an if at the end to keep only some items. This lets you filter a list while you build it.

blocks.caption: Keep only the items you want
blocks.code:
nums = [-3, 5, -1, 8, 0, 2]

# Keep only the positive numbers
positives = [x for x in nums if x > 0]
print("positives:", positives)

# Square only the even numbers
even_squares = [x * x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
print("even squares:", even_squares)
blocks.explain: The if at the end decides which items get in. positives keeps numbers > 0, and even_squares only squares the even ones.

blocks.question: What does [n + 1 for n in [10, 20, 30]] create?
blocks.explain: It does 'n + 1' for each n, so 10→11, 20→21, 30→31. The recipe is applied to every item.

blocks.heading: Dict comprehensions 🗝️
blocks.html: Lists aren't the only thing you can build this way. With curly braces { } and a key: value pair, you can build a whole dictionary in one line.

blocks.caption: Build a dictionary in one line
blocks.code:
words = ["cat", "tiger", "ox"]

# Map each word to its length
lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}
print(lengths)

# Map numbers to their squares
squares = {n: n * n for n in range(1, 5)}
print(squares)
blocks.explain: {w: len(w) for w in words} builds {'cat': 3, 'tiger': 5, 'ox': 2}. The part before the colon is the key, the part after is the value.

blocks.heading: Lambda: a tiny throwaway function
blocks.html:
A lambda is a mini function you write on one line, without a name. These two do exactly the same thing:

def double(x): return x * 2
double = lambda x: x * 2

Lambdas are handy when you need a quick little function for just one moment.

blocks.caption: lambda, and sorting with a key
blocks.code:
double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))

# Sort people by age using a lambda key
people = [("Sam", 12), ("Mia", 9), ("Leo", 15)]
by_age = sorted(people, key=lambda person: person[1])
print(by_age)
blocks.explain: double(5) gives 10. In sorted(), key=lambda person: person[1] tells Python to sort by the second item (the age), so youngest comes first.

blocks.html: When to reach for a comprehension: if your loop just builds a list or dict, a comprehension is cleaner. If it does lots of steps, a regular for loop is easier to read. Pick clarity first. 💡

blocks.heading: map() and filter() — and why comprehensions win
blocks.html: Python also has map() (change every item) and filter() (keep some items). They work, but most Python pros prefer comprehensions because they're easier to read. See for yourself. 👀

blocks.caption: Same result, cleaner code
blocks.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Old style with map() and filter()
doubled_map = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, nums))
evens_filter = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print("map:   ", doubled_map)
print("filter:", evens_filter)

# Pythonic style with comprehensions
doubled = [x * 2 for x in nums]
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
print("clean: ", doubled)
print("clean: ", evens)
blocks.explain: All four print the same results. The comprehension versions are shorter and read like plain English — that's why they win.

blocks.question: What is a lambda?
options: A small function written on one line, often without a name
blocks.explain: A lambda is a tiny anonymous function. lambda x: x * 2 is the same as a one-line def that doubles x.

blocks.question: Which one keeps only the big numbers from nums?
options: [x for x in nums if x > 100]
blocks.explain: The if x > 100 at the end is the filter — it only lets numbers bigger than 100 into the new list.

blocks.title: Triple every number
blocks.instructions: Use a list comprehension to make a new list where every number in nums is multiplied by 3. Print the result.
blocks.starter:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
# build 'tripled' with a comprehension
tripled = [___ for x in nums]
print(tripled)
blocks.solution:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
tripled = [x * 3 for x in nums]
print(tripled)
blocks.failMsg: Inside the brackets put x * 3, like [x * 3 for x in nums].

blocks.title: Filter out the small ones
blocks.instructions: Use a comprehension with an if to keep only the numbers greater than 10. Print the new list.
blocks.starter:
nums = [4, 15, 8, 23, 9, 42]
# keep only numbers > 10
big = [x for x in nums if ___]
print(big)
blocks.solution:
nums = [4, 15, 8, 23, 9, 42]
big = [x for x in nums if x > 10]
print(big)
blocks.passMsg: Nice filtering — you're writing pro-level code now! 💪
blocks.failMsg: Add the condition after if, like: [x for x in nums if x > 10].

challenge.title: Build a score report
challenge.instructions: You have a list of (name, score) pairs. 1) Use a dict comprehension to make a dictionary of name -> score. 2) Use sorted() with a lambda key to print the names sorted from highest score to lowest. Print the dictionary first, then the sorted names.
challenge.starter:
scores = [("Ana", 85), ("Ben", 92), ("Cara", 78)]
# 1) make a dict: name -> score
report = {___}
print(report)
# 2) sort names by score, highest first
ranked = sorted(scores, key=lambda pair: ___, reverse=True)
print([name for name, score in ranked])
challenge.solution:
scores = [("Ana", 85), ("Ben", 92), ("Cara", 78)]
report = {name: score for name, score in scores}
print(report)
ranked = sorted(scores, key=lambda pair: pair[1], reverse=True)
print([name for name, score in ranked])
challenge.hint: For the dict use {name: score for name, score in scores}. For sorting, the key is lambda pair: pair[1] and add reverse=True for highest first.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 A dict comprehension AND a lambda sort — that's seriously Pythonic. Day 20 complete!
